title: Overview
text: puzzle-widget is a Jupyter widget where students drag scrambled code lines into the correct order. Tag a cell %%puzzle <result>; every line below it becomes a draggable row. Drag a row (or focus it and press the arrow keys) to move it up or down – the widget re-runs the current order after every move and shows a checkmark once it produces <result>.

title: Overview
text:
Installation

pixicondapip


pixi workspace channel add munch-group
pixi add puzzle-widget


conda install -c munch-group puzzle-widget


pip install puzzle-widget




import puzzle_widget  # registers the %%puzzle cell magic

Below, the three lines are scrambled – a + b comes first, so running them in this order raises a NameError (a isn’t defined yet). Drag a = 10 and b = 5 above a + b (or focus a row and use the arrow keys) to fix the order; the widget shows “✓ Correct!” once it produces 15.

%%puzzle 15
a + b
b = 5
a = 10

title: Overview
text:
How it works
%%puzzle <result> parses <result> as a Python literal (ast.literal_eval) and splits the cell into non-blank, whitespace-stripped lines – each becomes one draggable row, in the order written. After every reorder, puzzle_widget.checker.run_puzzle executes the current order in a fresh, isolated namespace and reads off the value of the last line – which must be a bare expression, exactly like a notebook cell only shows output for a trailing expression, never for an assignment; the widget shows a success banner once that value equals <result>.
A puzzle’s lines must be independent, non-indented, single-line statements – no multi-line if/for/def/… blocks – so that every ordering at least parses; only the value produced should depend on getting the order right.

title: Overview
text:
Learning more
See the Examples page for a couple more puzzles.

title: checker.run_puzzle
text:
puzzle_widget.checker.run_puzzle(lines, expected)
Run lines (joined with newlines) in a fresh, isolated namespace and check whether the value of the last line equals expected.
Never raises: a SyntaxError from an unparseable order, or any exception raised while running the lines, is caught and reported via PuzzleResult.error with success=False – both are routine while a puzzle is still scrambled, not something callers need to guard against.



lines : list of str

The current candidate order, one statement per line.

expected : object

The target value (typically a literal parsed from the %%puzzle magic’s argument).






 : PuzzleResult

title: checker.run_puzzle
text:
lines : list of str

The current candidate order, one statement per line.

expected : object

The target value (typically a literal parsed from the %%puzzle magic’s argument).

title: PuzzleWidget
text:
puzzle_widget.PuzzleWidget(lines, expected)
A reorderable list of code lines that checks itself against an expected result.



lines : list of str

The puzzle’s lines in their initial (typically scrambled) order. Each must be a complete, independent, non-indented statement – no multi-line if/for/def/… blocks, since those can’t survive an arbitrary reordering (see puzzle_widget.checker).

expected : object

The value the lines should produce, in the correct order, to count as solved. Compared against the value of the last line, which must be a bare expression (e.g. a variable’s name on its own line) – an assignment produces no comparable value, exactly as it produces no cell output in a real notebook – see puzzle_widget.checker.run_puzzle.






success : bool

Whether the current order is correct. Recomputed automatically whenever lines changes (i.e. after every drag/keyboard reorder).

last_error : str or None

A short description of the exception raised by the current order, if any – expected and routine while the student is still arranging a scrambled puzzle, not shown in the widget UI itself.

success : bool

Whether the current order is correct. Recomputed automatically whenever lines changes (i.e. after every drag/keyboard reorder).

title: widget.register_puzzle_magic
text:
widget.register_puzzle_magic
puzzle_widget.widget.register_puzzle_magic(ipython=None)
Register the %%puzzle cell magic.
In IPython/Jupyter, %%puzzle <result> turns the cell’s lines into a reorderable PuzzleWidget that checks itself against <result> (a Python literal) after every reorder::
%%puzzle 15
a + b
b = 5
a = 10
<result> must be a literal (number, string, list, …) parsed with ast.literal_eval – not an arbitrary expression, since the puzzle runs in its own isolated namespace rather than the notebook’s, so a variable reference wouldn’t resolve to anything meaningful. Blank lines in the cell are dropped and each remaining line is stripped of surrounding whitespace before becoming a draggable row.
Called automatically on import; returns True when a live shell is found, False otherwise (e.g. plain Python).

title: Examples
text:
A couple more puzzles, one straight-line and one using a string method.
import puzzle_widget

title: Examples
text:
A slightly longer chain

%%puzzle 44
total = total + z
z = 2
total = x * y
total
y = 6
x = 7

title: Examples
text:
String methods

%%puzzle 'HELLO, WORLD'
greeting.upper()
greeting = 'hello, ' + name
name = 'world'
